Description
UMaine PhD Assistantship in Fish Environmental RNA (eRNA)

Institutions:  University of Maine, Orono, in collaboration with Dublin City University, Ireland and Queen’s University Belfast, North Ireland.

Responsibilities: The PhD student will advance research of an aquaculture environmental RNA (eRNA) project funded by the U.S. Department of Agriculture in collaboration with agencies in the Republic of Ireland and North Ireland. The project will investigate the scope and application of cutting-edge eRNA technology for assessing fish health from water samples. Specifically, the research will involve a combination of field, laboratory, and bioinformatic approaches aimed at characterizing eRNA in water from aquaculture settings for the assessment of Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar) disease, stress and development.  The student will be responsible for conducting fish environmental stress and pathogen exposure experiments, collecting water and tissue DNA and RNA samples, performing laboratory preparation and analysis of samples, analyzing associated bioinformatic datasets, and summarizing research in project reports, a dissertation, and peer-reviewed journal publications.
 Qualifications:  M.S. or extensive B.S./B.A. experience in fish biology, molecular genetics, bioinformatics or other relevant discipline(s) required. Additional quantitative skills or experiences in environmental DNA/RNA or aquaculture are desirable. A willingness to learn and attention to detail and research ethics are required. Potential for effective communication (written and verbal) and teamwork skills will be considered.

Compensation: Up to 4 years of PhD Research Assistantship stipend starting at $31,500 and increasing 3% each year, tuition, and half of graduate health insurance. There also may be opportunities for training and support under university Teaching Assistantships.
 
TO APPLY FOR THIS POSITION PLEASE COMPLETE THIS GOOGLE FORM, INCLUDING ATTACHED DOCUMENTS: https://forms.gle/ndyDAB4T3Ltw7QFF6
Please be aware that if you are selected, admission to graduate school at the University of Maine will include an additional institutional application process. 
 Application and Start Dates: Applications will be accepted until the position is filled, with initial review of the applicant pool beginning March 23rd, 2026. Anticipated position start date is as early as June 1, 2026 or as late as Sept 1, 2026. 

If you cannot fill out the Google application form or have further questions, please email mkinnison@maine.edu and/or erin.grey@maine.edu with the subject header "eRNA-Fish Assistantship"
 The University of Maine System is an EEO/AA employer, and does not discriminate on the grounds of race, color, religion, sex, sexual orientation, transgender status, gender expression, national origin, citizenship status, age, disability, genetic information or veteran’s status in employment, education, and all other programs and activities. The following person has been designated to handle inquiries regarding non-discrimination policies: Director of Equal Opportunity, 101 North Stevens Hall, University of Maine, Orono, ME 04469-5754, 207.581.1226, TTY 711 (Maine Relay System) or by email to: equal.opportunity@maine.edu
 

Contact
Erin Grey
erin.grey@maine.edu (preferred contact method)

Tags:
Graduate Opportunities
Description
MS Graduate Research Assistantship: Funded MS on voluntary wildlife protection, policy, and law on oil & gas concessions in the American West 

Laramie, WY

Job Title: MS Graduate Research Assistantship, University of Wyoming

Location: Laramie, WY 

Overview: Dr. Kelly Dunning & Associate Dean Professor Temple Stoellinger are recruiting a student to join the Wildlife & Wilderness Recreation Lab in the Haub School of Environment and Natural Resources at the University of Wyoming. 

The Wildlife & Wilderness Recreation Lab works on sustainable nature-based tourism, hunting and fishing, and outdoor recreation issues in Wyoming and the Intermountain West. Our research focuses on public policy of wildlife, recreation, and natural resources making our research practical and impactful to resource management. The MS student will work on a project studying voluntary conservation activities for species like elk, deer, and grouse on oil and gas concessions. The MS student will have interdisciplinary opportunities to provide support to the Wildlife & Wilderness Recreation Lab as needed (please see: wildlifewildernessrecreationlab.org). Our lab has experience working with students of diverse backgrounds, veterans, and non-conventional students, and all are welcome to apply. 

Duties: The Graduate Research Assistant will focus on voluntary conservation activities and relevant conservation laws and policies for species protections on oil and gas concessions on both private land and leases of federal and state public land. This position is ideal for people who want to work in conservation with insights that are valuable to the private sector. This pragmatic focus allows you to follow political priorities of the current administration and be competitive on the job market.  

Ideal candidates for this position will possess outstanding written and oral communication skills, an interest in conservation, and a willingness to write research independently. 

Candidates should have experience 1) working as part of a team on conservation social science, wildlife, or environmental politics and policy in the United States or similar topics, 2) conducting research or outreach in natural or social science. Students with prior work experience in sectors like service or retail are encouraged to list these experiences on their resume as well as it demonstrates independence and ability to work with the public. Candidates should have an interest in fish and wildlife in the Intermountain West, oil and gas, wilderness recreation, and/or an appreciation for federal land recreation and tourism. Candidates should show in their cover letter examples of their ability to work independently. Preferred candidates will have experience with 1) wildlife science or social science such as hunter or angler surveys, political science/public policy/economics, 2) statistical analysis, 3) social science methods/surveys/human dimensions/interview methodologies, 4) scientific or academic writing. Students must possess a valid drivers license and be willing and able to conduct field work in rural communities that may require significant time spent outdoors and in nature. 

Start Date: Fall 2026 in Laramie, Wyoming 

End Date: An MS in the Wildlife & Wilderness Recreation Lab requires 5 semesters of field work and courses ending at the conclusion of spring term 2027 (fall ‘26, spring ‘27, summer ‘27, fall ‘27, spring ‘28). Dunning and Stoellinger will provide close supervision and career planning support to ensure completion on time. 

Degree information: The candidate(s) selected will pursue their MS through the University of Wyoming’s Environment, Natural Resources, and Society program (ENRS). A funded MS covers required credits of tuition and fees during the fall/spring/summer semesters and student health insurance. 

Qualifications: Preference will be given to applicants with interest and/or experience in hunting/fishing surveys, outdoor recreation, and/or interdisciplinary research. Applicants should have a bachelor's degree and a strong positive professional reference from a few previous supervisors. Applicants who have a BA or BS in any field will be considered, with preference given to social science, fish and wildlife science, natural resources, political science, business, or interdisciplinary fields. Applicants must have an undergraduate GPA of 3.0 or higher. Standardized test scores (e.g., GRE) are not required although they can be used to supplement weaker applications. Candidates from all backgrounds are encouraged to apply. Veterans and spouse/children dependents are encouraged to apply. Legal right to work in the United States will need to be held by the applicant. Formal application to the UW ENRS program is required subsequent to selection of the successful candidate(s). 

Questions: Questions may be asked about the vacancy to Kelly Dunning via email, but all relevant information needed to apply to the vacancies is included in the above description. Due to the extremely high volume of interest of previous positions in the lab, feedback on individual applications is not possible and individual conversations prior to submission of application materials is not possible. 

Funding Package: The stipend for Fall 2026 through summer 2026 will be $23,000 (summer funding is included); the stipend for Fall 2026 through May 2027 will be $16,938. tuition, and health insurance. Graduation is May 2027.  

Application Deadline: March 13, 2026 at 5 p.m. MST, Apply early as review of applications will be on a rolling basis, and positions like this are often filled long before the closing date. 

How to apply: Please send the following materials as one PDF document to Dr. Kelly Dunning at kelly.dunning@uwyo.edu with the subject “Your Name MS Position”. This packet should include as a single combined PDF:
(1) Cover letter/narrative (roughly 1-2 pages) that explains your relevant skills and experience identifying specific requirements outlined in this position description, why you want to study at the Haub School, career goals, and your ability to work independently.  
(2) Resume or CV including degree(s) earned, GPA, and contact information for at least three references 
(3) an unofficial copy of your transcript
(4) a writing sample close to the focus of this MS this can be of any length and on any topic

Next steps: Finalists will be contacted for an interview and an optional short writing task to assess the applicant’s writing skills
Contact
Kelly Dunning
kelly.dunning@uwyo.edu (preferred contact method)

title: M.S. Assistantship – Habitat use, movements, and survival of female Mottled Ducks
description:
M.S. Assistantship – Habitat use, movements, and survival of female Mottled Ducks
Tennessee Technological University (State) | https://www.cohenwildlifelab.com/
Details
Application Deadline:
04/01/2026
Published:
02/26/2026
Starting Date:
after 8/1/2026
Hours per Week:
at least 20
Salary:
starting at $22,000 per year
Education Required:
Bachelors
Experience Required:
none
Location:
Cookeville, Tennessee
Tags:
Graduate Opportunities
Description
We are seeking applicants for an M.S. assistantship focused on mottled duck (Anas fulvigula) ecology and management.  Available survey data suggest western Gulf Coast mottled ducks have experienced a long-term population decline, and the species has been identified as a focal species by the Louisiana Department of Wildlife & Fisheries, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, and Gulf Coast Joint Venture. This project will involve deploying GPS/GSM implant transmitters on female mottled ducks across the Chenier and Deltaic Plains of Louisiana to evaluate habitat selection and movement characteristics during the breeding and nonbreeding seasons, quantify how movements and habitat selection contribute to survival and nest success, identify habitat characteristics at nesting sites, and assess season-specific survival and cause-specific mortality. The selected student will work in close collaboration with the Louisiana Department of Wildlife and Fisheries, Ducks Unlimited, and other regional conservation partners. The successful applicant will receive a $22,000 per year stipend and tuition and fee waiver. The student will be advised by Dr. Bradley Cohen to start fall semester 2026. Applications can be sent to Bradley Cohen (bcohen@tntech.edu) until 4/1/2026 and should include a brief cover letter, a CV, contact information for 3 resumes, and unofficial transcripts. 
Contact
Bradley Cohen
bcohen@tntech.edu (preferred contact method)

Graduate Student Scholarships and Work Study Positions for MS Students
Antioch University (Private) | https://www.antioch.edu/academics/environmental-studies-sustainability/environmental-studies-ms/conservation-biology-es-ms/

Salary:
starting at $16 per hour
Education Required:
Bachelors
Experience Required:
none
Location:
remote work allowed
Tags:
Graduate Opportunities
Description
Antioch University is offering partial scholarships and work study positions, if you enroll in the Environmental Studies Master’s program, and is accepting applications to start your MS degree during the summer or fall semester of 2026. MS Concentrations include Conservation Biology, Environmental Education, and Advocacy for Social Justice and Sustainability. In Conservation Biology, students focus on gaining quantitative (GIS and statistics), writing, and ecological field skills. Students also gain experience with community engagement, social justice, and applied research and service. 

Students can earn their graduate degree with either face-to-face classes at the Antioch University New England campus in Keene, NH, USA, or fully online classes (that can be taken from anywhere) which are offered each semester, or with a mixture of the two. Ecological field trip classes (intensive 1-2 week trips to destinations abroad and in-country) are also available for all students. All classes are just 1-2 days a week, making this a viable program for environmental professionals working part or full-time. Our programs are student-centered, flexible, and tailored to meet your individual needs, learning style, and location.

Students often work with Antioch faculty in our existing research programs. Specifically in our Conservation Biology concentration, we have expertise in vertebrate ecology (e.g., birds, snakes), invertebrate ecology (e.g., estuarine invertebrates, insects), tropical ecology, forest ecology, alpine ecology, marine ecology, and coastal restoration. In some cases, faculty may have extramural funding to support students in seasonal field and lab positions, including research in New England and in the Caribbean. Students who are eligible for US federal work-study can also work part time in various on-campus work-study positions, including Graduate Teaching and Research Assistantships and various positions in our numerous campus centers (e.g., Institute for International Conservation, Spatial Analysis Lab). However, you need to be accepted and taking classes as a graduate student to be eligible for these work-study positions. Students at Antioch generally pay for at least part of their own tuition, but we offer numerous university-wide grants and scholarships, which can help defray tuition costs, and which are detailed here: https://www.antioch.edu/admission-aid/financial-aid/scholarships-and-grants/ . We also work with students to help them apply for external scholarships, fellowships, and research-focused grants.

Join our tight-knit community as we strive to advance social and environmental justice, conserve biodiversity, and mitigate climate change as a department and as a university! For more information about our programs, visit our webpages (https://www.antioch.edu/academics/program-finder?_sft_focus=environmental-studies-sustainability ,  https://www.antioch.edu/academics/environmental-studies-sustainability/environmental-studies-ms/conservation-biology-es-ms/ ) or contact Melissa Baptiste, Admissions Counselor, at mbaptiste (at) antioch.edu. 

Contact
Melissa Baptiste
mbaptiste@antioch.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
The Center for Wildlife Sustainability Research, Southern Illinois University Carbondale is inviting applications for a PhD level Graduate Assistantship beginning 17 August 2026.  The successful candidate will begin studies with a Teaching Assistantship for the first 1.5 years at a salary of 1,805/month, then transfer to a Research Assistantship at a salary of 2,518/month. This assistantship is on a 12-month basis and include a full tuition waiver and approximately $200,000/year to support for research activities. The student will be part of an ongoing 10-year project studying the influence of grassland plant communities on grassland dependent wildlife communities with an emphasis on avian reproductive success. 
 Graduate studies will lead to a PhD degree in Zoology or Ecology at Southern Illinois University Carbondale.  Competitive candidates will have completed a Bachelor’s and Master’s degree in Wildlife Ecology, Zoology, or a related field.  Demonstrated ability to identify passerines by sight and sound and conduct rigorous field work under extreme conditions will be necessary.  Previous experience or a desire to continue a career in avian ecology is desirable, but not essential.
            Applicants should provide a letter of interest that includes a brief biological sketch and career goals, a resume, GRE scores if available, and GPA. I will review applications as they are received and continue until the position is filled. Application packets will be provided to candidates selected for further consideration. 
Contact
Mike Eichholz
6184536951
eichholz@siu.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
M.S. Graduate Research Assistantship — Crayfish Population Ecology (Fall 2026)
Texas A&M University–Kingsville | Aquatic Ecology Lab (Dr. Zachary Mitchell)

A funded M.S. graduate research assistantship is available beginning August 2026 to support research on Texas crayfish ecology and conservation. This position is part of a larger project examining the distribution, habitat use, environmental tolerances, and population dynamics of imperiled Texas crayfish species.

The selected student will focus specifically on the population monitoring and mark–recapture component of two SGCN crayfish species. Responsibilities will include intensive field sampling, tagging and recapture efforts, demographic analyses, and interpretation of environmental drivers of population dynamics. Work will involve regular field travel across South Texas and substantial data analysis.

Funding and Duration
The assistantship provides $2,000/month (+ fringe benefits) for 24 months. A non-resident tuition waiver is available for out-of-state students (resident tuition and fees still apply).

Qualifications
Applicants should be comfortable working outdoors in challenging field conditions. Experience with aquatic ecology, crayfish, fisheries, or macroinvertebrate sampling is preferred but not required. Strong interest in quantitative ecology, conservation, or freshwater ecosystems is required.

Application Details
The position will remain open until filled, but full consideration will be given to applications received by March 13, 2026.

Please email the following as a single PDF to:
zachary.mitchell@tamuk.edu

Include:
 • Cover letter describing research interests and experience
 • Curriculum vitae or résumé

Prospective applicants are encouraged to visit my lab website (aqautic-ecology.org) to learn more about ongoing research and lab philosophy.

Contact
Zachary Mitchell
zachary.mitchell@tamuk.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
Habitat/Spatial Ecology Lecturer Position

The Department of Wildlife at Cal Poly Humboldt is seeking a lecturer with expertise in Habitat Ecology, Spatial Ecology, or Landscape Ecology for the 2026-2027 academic year. The successful candidate will teach Ecology & Management of Habitat for Wildlife and Spatial Wildlife Ecology, two upper-division courses in the undergraduate curriculum, and/or other courses as assigned.  The position is full-time, though a part-time appointment may be possible if desired. Potential additional course topics include: introduction to Wildlife Conservation and Policy, Ecology of Wildlife Populations, and/or Senior Project (see here for course offerings). To learn more about the department, please visit our website. Initial appointment will be for the Fall 2026 semester and subsequent reappointments will be contingent upon satisfactory performance evaluations, and programmatic need. The department has a strong record of multi-year instructional continuity.
 QUALIFICATIONS: PhD (or ABD) in Wildlife, Ecology, or a related field from an accredited college. Candidates must have demonstrated topical expertise in the subject area and/or demonstrated teaching experience at the university or collegial level. Preferred qualifications are a record of training and expertise in spatial ecology and/or habitat ecology, with additional experience in areas such as wildlife management, ecology and evolution.

SALARY: See information on website

APPLICATION: Qualified candidates should submit the following materials through PageUp application portal: 

Letter of Application; 
Curriculum Vitae; 
Teaching Evaluations, if available; and 
Names and Contact Information for Three (3) Professional References. 

Questions may be directed to: Department Chair Barbara Clucas – barbara.clucas@humboldt.edu

APPLICATION DEADLINE: Review of applications will begin March 20. Early response is encouraged. 
Contact
Barbara Clucas
707-826-5651
barbara.clucas@humboldt.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
Description:
The Environmental Institute of Houston at the University of Houston–Clear Lake is seeking a research associate to conduct environmental and biological research. The position assists with project design, field data collection, data entry and analysis, report preparation, and dissemination of results. Duties include maintaining and operating sampling equipment, handling research vessels, leading small teams of research staff and students, and using specialized data management, processing, and analysis programs. 

 *Note: This is a grant funded position. Continuation of the position is dependent upon future funding.

**In the application materials, please also address any specific experience with green-infrastructure projects, geospatial science, wetland delineation, soil science, or water quality monitoring.

Duties:
Collect environmental and biological data associated with various projects.
Maintain, calibrate, and operate field and laboratory environmental and biological data collection instruments and equipment including vessels and vehicles.
Lead small teams of research staff and students in the collection of environmental and biological data collection.
Participate in and oversee data entry, quality control, and basic analysis of data and report compilation and perform related duties as assigned.

Qualifications:
Required
Bachelor's degree in Environmental Science, Biology, Marine Biology, or a related field.
Experience working in a full-time position conducting environmental/biological research. (Experience gained through academic programs is applicable.)
Valid Driver's License

Preferred 
Master's degree in Environmental Science, Biology, Marine Biology, or a related field.
Two years of experience working in a full-time position conducting environmental/biological research.
 Additional Notes: All positions at the University of Houston-System are security sensitive and will require a criminal history and a motor vehicle background check.
The University of Houston System and its universities are Equal Opportunity Institutions. Everyone is encouraged to apply.

ANTICIPATED START DATE:    As soon as possible (dependent on completion of hiring paperwork)

APPLICATION DEADLINE:        Until filled

HOW TO APPLY: Interested candidates must access the full job posting and apply through the following link: https://careers.uh.edu/jobs/research-associate-houston-texas-united-states.  Candidates should plan to submit a cover letter, current resume, and contact info for three references. 
  Contact
Mandi Gordon
gordon@uhcl.edu (preferred contact method)

Tags:

Description

Title: Kinship of warm water non-natives in the Grand Canyon
Location: New Mexico State University; Las Cruces, New Mexico (remote work arrangements may be considered)
Job category: Postdoctoral researcher
Salary: $63-70K/year dependent on experience and benefits, 
Start date: Fall semester 2026 (~Aug 17)
Position duration: 18 months, but potential opportunity to extend
Deadline to apply: 03/25/26 by 11:59p MT

Description:
The Pregler Lab at New Mexico State University in the Department of Fish, Wildlife, and Conservation Ecology invites applications for a postdoctoral research position focused on kinship analysis to manage non-native species in the Grand Canyon. The successful applicant will begin in the Fall 2026 semester (~08/17/26).

The purpose of this project is to use kinship genetic analysis to better understand the ongoing expansion of smallmouth bass (SMB) into the Grand Canyon. Non-native SMB were first discovered below Lake Powell in 2022, and genetic samples were taken from captured individuals each year since then to better understand their expansion downriver to inform management efforts. Management efforts to date have included cold-water releases from the Glen Canyon Dam to limit SMB reproduction and recruitment.

The successful candidate will use kinship analyses (including parentage and close-kin mark recapture, CKMR) on genetic data from this system to better understand reproduction and abundance of SMB. Results from this effort will provide important information on the reproductive dynamics and larval sources for a high-risk species that has the potential to prey on native species if it disperses downstream. These data will inform discussions of management strategies, current stage of invasion, and will provide information on the effectiveness of implemented flow experiments to disadvantage recruitment and survival of smallmouth bass. 

Objectives:
1.   Use kinship genetic analysis to understand the expansion of smallmouth bass in the Grand Canyon.
2.   Identify if juveniles captured downstream are locally produced or entrained from Lake Powell.
3.   Estimate abundance and reproduction using parentage and close-kin mark recapture (CKMR) methods.

 Qualifications:
Minimum qualifications:
1.   PhD degree by start date in Fisheries, Ecology, or related field by the anticipated start date (preferred fall 2026 semester).
2.   Expertise in R coding language.
3.   Demonstrated ability to publish in peer-reviewed journals.
4.   Excellent written and personal communication skills.
5.   The ability to work both independently and collaboratively, and to meet deadlines.
 Desired qualifications:
Competitive candidates will have experience in one or more of the following areas: conservation genetics analysis, kinship/parentage analysis, close-kin mark recapture, population ecology and abundance estimation.
 This project is a collaborative effort funded by the USGS Southwest Science Center and Bureau of Reclamation and there may be opportunities to collaborate with other university researchers and agencies. This position is initially funded for 18 months, and the successful candidate will be hired through New Mexico State University and be based in Las Cruces, NM although remote options may be considered. The preferred start date is at the beginning of the fall 2026 semester (~8/17/26).
 The postdoctoral research will be provided with an annual salary ranging between $63,000 and $70,000 depending on experience, plus benefits, a desktop computer (based at NMSU) and travel funds to attend conferences.
 How to apply:
Applicants are asked to submit the following: 
           1. A writing sample (e.g., peer-reviewed manuscript, dissertation draft),
           2. A compiled pdf that includes the following:
                    (a)  a cover letter that describes your qualifications, research interests and how this position fits into your career goals, 
                    (b)  curriculum vitae,
                    (c)  contact information for three professional references, 
                    (d)  unofficial transcripts 
                    (e)  compiled in a single PDF and save as ‘Lastname_kinship_postdoc.pdf’. 

Applications should be submitted through the portal linked here: https://airtable.com/appm3dwqIB8V8POFh/pag8qi16jzEDVcUG1/form 

Applications are due 03/25/26 by 11:59p MT. You can learn more about the Pregler Lab at https://preglerlab.com and any questions about the position can be sent to Dr. Kasey Pregler at kpregler@nmsu.edu.   

Contact
Kasey Pregler
kpregler@nmsu.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
Position: Research Assistant (Student Services Contractor)
Location: Lake Erie Biological Station, Huron, OH 
Salary: $21.94–33.24 per hour (qualification-dependent); Approximately 40 hours per week. 

Duration of position: This is a contract position projected to begin May 11, 2026. The intended duration is one year (May 11, 2026–May 10, 2027) with possible extension dependent on future funding. 

Project: Understanding Grass Carp Ecology to Inform Control Efforts in the Great Lakes

Position Description: The student contractor will serve as a contracted Research Assistant supporting field-based research on various aspects of Grass Carp ecology in the Great Lakes, including but not limited to, reproductive biology, early life history, foraging ecology, impacts on aquatic vegetation, movement ecology, and early detection. The contractor will be based at the Lake Erie Biological Station in Huron, OH and play a critical support role for USGS research by assisting with a variety of duties that may include, but are not limited to: 

• Collecting fish eggs and larvae in rivers using boat-mounted ichthyoplankton nets
• Processing of ichthyoplankton samples and preparing samples for genetic analysis
•Assisting in the collection fish and plant tissue samples for stable isotope analysis
•Supporting movement research by preparing and deploying telemetry equipment
•Collecting and recording environmental data (e.g., water quality) related to sampling events
•Using hydroacoustics equipment (e.g., acoustic doppler current profiler) to measure river flow •Entering data and reviewing data quality using Microsoft Excel and/or a programming language
• Assisting in creating, updating, and refining various scientific reports and publications
•Participating in other research activities at the station, as needed

Education/Experience Requirements: The successful candidate must have: 
• A bachelor’s or master's degree in fisheries science, biology, ecology, computer science, engineering (hydrology/hydrodynamics focus), and/or data science or a related field that was awarded less than one year prior to beginning the contract
• A valid U.S. driver’s license
• Experience with R or another programming language is preferred, but not required 
• Great attention to detail, desire to learn, and the ability to work in extreme weather conditions
• High motivation with the ability to multi-task and work collaboratively in a team setting
•Flexibility to work early mornings, nights, and weekends on occasion is preferred, as fieldwork occurs in response to rainfall events

Other Details: This contract covers a 52-week period with an anticipated start date of May 11, 2026. The student contractor will not receive holiday pay, annual leave, or health care benefits, nor will they receive overtime pay for hours worked in excess of 80 within a two-week period. Hours in excess of the established 80 per two weeks will be paid at the regular hourly rate. The student will report to the Lake Erie Biological Station in Huron, OH, for all duties, unless otherwise specified. Office work will occur in an office equipped with a computer workstation. Moving expenses will not be paid. The selected candidate will be required to register in the System for Award Management (SAM).

The successful candidate will be hired as an independent contractor. This is not a government position, and time does not count toward time-in-service. 

Review of applications will begin immediately and continue until filled. To ensure consideration of your application, please send a cover letter, resume or CV, and a list of two professional references to Corbin Hilling (chilling@usgs.gov, 419-365-6492).
Contact
Corbin Hilling
419-365-6492
chilling@usgs.gov (preferred contact method)

University of Wisconsin - Madison (State)

03/31/2026
Published:
02/23/2026
Starting Date:
after 5/1/2026
Hours per Week:
40
Salary:
starting at $62,232 per year
Education Required:
Doctorate
Experience Required:
none
Location:
Madison, Wisconsin
Tags:
Faculty / Post-Doc Appointments
Description
Postdoctoral Research Associate: Soil Microbial Genomics & Biogeochemistry
 Position Overview
 We are looking for a postdoctoral researcher to take the lead on the molecular and bioinformatic components of a project examining how soil microbes respond to changes in resource availability in dryland ecosystems.
 The successful candidate will work with a large, integrated dataset including metagenomes, metatranscriptomes, and metabolomes, to better understand how microbial communities in the Chihuahuan Desert shift in response to changes in carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and phosphorus (P) availability. This work will help improve predictions of how dryland carbon cycling and ecosystem processes may respond to global environmental change.
 The appointment with be housed at the University of Wisconsin-Madison with Dr. Kristina Young in the Integrative Biology Department. Salaries follow NIH salary guidelines: https://www.niaid.nih.gov/grants-contracts/salary-cap-stipends. Appointment length is one year, with the possibility of renewal.
 Project Background
 Drylands make up more than 40% of the Earth’s land surface and play large roles in the global carbon cycle. Yet we still lack a clear understanding of what controls soil microbial carbon cycling and carbon use efficiency (CUE) in these water-limited, nutrient-poor systems.
 This project builds on a completed laboratory incubation and carbon/nutrient addition experiment using a full factorial design (27 C:N:P treatments) applied to dryland soils from the Chihuahuan Desert. The postdoctoral researcher will analyze sequencing data to evaluate how carbon and nutrient additions influence gene expression and microbial community composition from these under-studied dryland soils.
 Key Responsibilities
-  Multi-Omics Analysis: Lead the analysis of metagenomic, metatranscriptomic, and metabolomic datasets 
-  Assemble and bin short-read sequences to generate metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs), and map transcript reads to determine taxon-specific activity.
-  Integrate transcriptomic and metabolomic data to identify indicators of microbial metabolic activity.
- Relate multi-omics data to soil biogeochemical measurements
-  Lead manuscript preparation and publication. 
-  Mentorship: Support and mentor graduate and undergraduate students to the extent desired by the research associate 
 Qualifications
Required
Ph.D. in Microbial Ecology, Soil Science, Bioinformatics, Microbiology, or a related discipline
Strong command-line experience (Bash, Python, R) and familiarity with high-performance computing environments
Experience working with omics datasets, particularly metagenomics and/or metatranscriptomics
Knowledge of microbial biogeochemistry, especially C, N, and P cycling
Demonstrated record of scientific writing and publication
 Preferred
Familiarity with response surface models or multivariate approaches (e.g., PLS)
Background in dryland or terrestrial ecosystem ecology
Experience working with metabolite data
 What the project offers:
- Immediate access to a high-quality dataset including well-validated RNA samples. 
- Collaboration with researchers from UW-Madison, USDA-ARS, USGS Southwest Biological Science Center, LLNL, and UTEP.
-  Engagement with the NSF Dryland Critical Zone Thematic Cluster and research at the Jornada Long-Term Ecological Research (LTER) site, contributing to improved global carbon modeling efforts.
-  Opportunities to engage with a wide postdoc community at UW-Madison: https://postdoc.wisc.edu/
 How to Apply
 Please submit a cover letter describing your experience with bioinformatics and your interest in this position, along with a current CV and contact information for three professional references to Dr. Kristina Young at Kristina.young@wisc.edu. 
  Contact
Kristina Young
kristina.young@wisc.edu (preferred contact method)

-  Engagement with the NSF Dryland Critical Zone Thematic Cluster and research at the Jornada Long-Term Ecological Research (LTER) site, contributing to improved global carbon modeling efforts.
-  Opportunities to engage with a wide postdoc community at UW-Madison: https://postdoc.wisc.edu/
 How to Apply
 Please submit a cover letter describing your experience with bioinformatics and your interest in this position, along with a current CV and contact information for three professional references to Dr. Kristina Young at Kristina.young@wisc.edu.
